skill.name: devops-iac-engineer
skill.description: Guides DevOps and platform teams through Terraform, Kubernetes, CI/CD, cloud architecture, observability, and DevSecOps implementation.
skill.summary: Build and review secure infrastructure as code workflows for cloud and Kubernetes platforms.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and filesystem patterns, but most reviewed items are documentation snippets or DevOps examples. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration path was found. Medium risk remains because the package includes an executable helper that can read or write local paths, plus examples that include placeholder secrets and broad network settings.

medium_findings.title: Local Utility Reads and Writes User-Controlled Paths
medium_findings.description: The bundled Python utility creates Terraform project files from a user supplied project name and scans a user supplied directory for secrets. This is legitimate DevOps functionality, but it can write outside the intended workspace or expose local secret snippets if run with unsafe arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly uses user supplied paths for file creation and recursive scanning. There is no network exfiltration, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Executable Terraform Command Wrapper
medium_findings.description: The utility invokes terraform fmt against a user supplied file path. The subprocess call uses an argument list rather than a shell, which lowers injection risk, but running the helper still executes a local external command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is real and reachable through the CLI. The arguments are not passed through a shell, so command injection evidence is limited.

medium_findings.title: Production-Looking Examples Include Placeholder Secrets and Open Network Rules
medium_findings.description: Example Kubernetes and Terraform files include placeholder secret values and internet-wide CIDR rules. These examples are common for DevOps templates, but users could deploy them unchanged if they miss the placeholders.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are visibly placeholders or standard example CIDRs, not evidence of embedded real credentials. The risk is that production-looking examples may be copied without hardening.

low_findings.title: Most Static Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command detections occur inside Markdown guides, pipeline examples, and Makefile templates. They document normal DevOps workflows and do not execute automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed instances are presented as Markdown examples or templates rather than hidden runtime behavior. Users still need normal caution before running copied commands.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Targeted review for override language, fake system instructions, approval claims, and instructions to skip security analysis found no evidence in the reviewed skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The primary skill instructions were reviewed and a targeted text search found no suspicious override phrases. This supports dismissal of prompt injection concerns for the inspected content.

low_findings.title: Static Secret and Terraform State Hits Are Mostly Preventive Guidance
low_findings.description: Several detections occur in sections that teach users not to hardcode credentials or to ignore sensitive files. These are security education examples, not embedded operational secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context frames these strings as bad examples, ignore patterns, or secret detector regexes. No real secret value or exfiltration destination was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Local Secret Scanner
dangerous_patterns.description: The optional utility walks a user supplied directory and prints partial matches for secret patterns. This can be helpful locally but may disclose sensitive material in terminal logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recursive scan and printed findings are explicit in the script. The behavior is local and user initiated, so it is not evidence of malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsanitized Project Path Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The Terraform scaffolding command uses the supplied project name as a filesystem path. Absolute paths or traversal values could create files outside the expected working directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path is derived directly from CLI input and then used for mkdir and write_text calls. The function is still an explicit scaffolding tool, not hidden behavior.

content.value_statement: Infrastructure teams need repeatable cloud deployments without missing reliability or security controls. This skill provides Terraform, Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability, and DevSecOps guidance for practical implementation.
actual_capabilities: Explains Terraform module structure, state management, provider setup, and environment patterns.
Provides Kubernetes manifest guidance for deployments, services, ingress, probes, resources, and autoscaling.
Describes CI/CD and GitOps workflows for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, ArgoCD, and Flux.
Includes observability guidance for metrics, logs, traces, dashboards, alerts, and SLOs.
Offers DevSecOps practices for secrets handling, policy as code, image scanning, and compliance checks.
Includes a Python utility script for Terraform project scaffolding, manifest validation, GitOps setup, and secret scanning.
limitations: Generated infrastructure must be reviewed against current cloud provider documentation before deployment.
Example manifests contain placeholders and must not be used unchanged in production.
The bundled script can read and write local files when run by the user.
The skill does not verify live cloud account permissions, quotas, costs, or compliance status.

use_cases.title: Create a Terraform Foundation
use_cases.description: Design reusable modules, environments, remote state, and provider configuration for a new cloud project.

use_cases.title: Review Kubernetes Deployment Readiness
use_cases.description: Check manifests for probes, resource limits, service accounts, ingress, autoscaling, and secret management concerns.

use_cases.title: Design a Delivery Pipeline
use_cases.description: Plan build, test, scan, deploy, rollback, and GitOps stages for a containerized service.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the IaC Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an infrastructure request
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the infrastructure needed for this application. Include Terraform resources, Kubernetes components, security controls, and deployment steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Terraform Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Terraform project structure for AWS with dev, staging, and prod environments. Include modules, remote state, variables, outputs, and review checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review Kubernetes Manifests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Kubernetes manifests for production readiness. Check security context, probes, resources, networking, secrets, autoscaling, and observability.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Secure GitOps Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitOps deployment workflow for this service. Include CI stages, image scanning, policy checks, progressive delivery, rollback, monitoring, and access controls.

output_examples.input: A team needs a new multi-environment Terraform layout for a web application.
output: A recommended folder structure for modules and environments.
Remote state and locking guidance for each environment.
Security and cost review points before the first apply.

output_examples.input: A Kubernetes deployment is missing production safeguards.
output: A readiness checklist covering probes, resources, service accounts, and network policy.
Secret management recommendations using sealed or external secrets.
Observability additions for metrics, logs, alerts, and rollout checks.

output_examples.input: A service needs a CI/CD pipeline with container scanning and staged deployment.
output: A staged pipeline outline from build through production rollout.
Quality gates for tests, image scanning, and infrastructure validation.
Rollback and monitoring steps for failed releases.

best_practices: Replace all placeholder secrets, domains, CIDRs, and account values before deployment.
Run generated infrastructure through formatting, validation, security scanning, and peer review.
Keep Terraform state encrypted, access controlled, and separated by environment.
anti_patterns: Do not apply example infrastructure directly to production without review.
Do not commit real secrets, kubeconfigs, Terraform state, or cloud credentials to source control.
Do not run bundled utilities against sensitive directories unless you understand the local file access.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy infrastructure automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and an optional local utility script. Users control any execution.

faq.question: Can it help with Terraform modules?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers module structure, variables, outputs, state, providers, and environment separation.

faq.question: Can it review Kubernetes manifests?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review manifests for readiness, security settings, resources, probes, and observability.

faq.question: Are the included secrets real?
faq.answer: No. The visible secrets are placeholders, but they still require replacement before any deployment.

faq.question: Does it support multiple cloud providers?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, and multi-cloud Terraform patterns.

faq.question: What should users check before using generated IaC?
faq.answer: Users should verify costs, permissions, regions, network exposure, secrets, compliance rules, and provider versions.
